title: China Trains Frontier AI Without Nvidia, FERC Orders Grids to Open for Data Centers
subhead: Meituan open-sources a 1.6-trillion-parameter model built entirely on domestic chips — the strongest signal yet that China's GPU substitution is becoming viable. Meanwhile, FERC unanimously orders grid operators to accelerate data center connections, and Congo tightens its grip on global cobalt supply.

sections.heading: Meituan's LongCat-2.0: A Frontier Model Without Nvidia
sections.body:
China's Meituan open-sourced LongCat-2.0, a 1.6-trillion-parameter mixture-of-experts model that the company says was trained end-to-end on domestic Chinese ASIC clusters — specifically, a 50,000-chip superpod. It's the largest model claimed to be trained entirely without Nvidia hardware. (SiliconANGLE; The Next Web)

The model uses a MoE architecture similar to DeepSeek-R1, with a 1-million-token context window and benchmarks pitched against Gemini, GPT-5.5, and Claude Opus. Meituan released it under the MIT license, positioning it for agentic coding and task-orchestration workflows. (SiliconANGLE; VentureBeat)

The domestic-chip claim matters because China's access to Nvidia's most powerful GPUs has been intermittently restricted since 2022 US export controls. Bernstein estimated in 2025 that Nvidia held roughly 40% of China's AI chip market, matched by Huawei — and projected Nvidia's share would fall 8 points this year. Meituan's demonstration that a near-frontier model can be trained on Chinese hardware alone is the most concrete proof point yet that the substitution curve is real, not aspirational. (SiliconANGLE; AP via Memeburn)
sections.whyItMatters: If China can train frontier models on domestic hardware at scale, US export controls on GPUs lose their primary lever. The question shifts from 'can China substitute?' to 'how fast does the substitution accelerate?'

sections.heading: FERC Unanimously Orders Grid Operators to Speed Data Center Connections
sections.body:
FERC voted unanimously on June 18 to direct six regional grid operators — covering 200 million Americans — to ensure AI data centers and other large power users can connect to transmission systems 'in a timely and orderly manner.' Chair Laura Swett called the vote 'historic.' (AP News)

The order requires grid operators to respond within 30 days on adequate power supply plans and 60 days on integration plans for large loads. Data centers must pay the full cost of grid upgrades needed for their connection. (AP News)

The same week, FERC's order bolstered Maryland's case that households have been unlawfully billed $1.6 billion for transmission upgrades serving out-of-state data centers in Virginia. Eighty Maryland lawmakers pressed FERC for immediate relief from $2 billion in cost-shifting by PJM Interconnection. (Southern Maryland Chronicle)

Separately, in Q1 2026, ERCOT received proposals from nearly 200 data centers seeking 438 GW of capacity in Texas alone. (Dallas Morning News)
sections.whyItMatters: The FERC order tries to square two conflicting pressures: speed data center buildout for AI competitiveness, and prevent residential ratepayers from subsidizing tech giants. Whether it can do both depends on enforcement, which is undefined.

sections.heading: Congo Forfeits Unused Cobalt Export Quotas, Tightens Supply Grip
sections.body:
DRC's strategic minerals regulator ARECOMS announced on June 30 that all cobalt export quotas allocated for January–June that remain unused will be forfeited and reassigned to the state's 'strategic quota' as of July 1. Only shipments declared in customs by July 5 qualify under first-half allocations. (Mining Weekly)

Congo holds over 70% of global cobalt reserves. Cobalt prices have surged 160% since February 2025 to $57,320/tonne as export curbs squeeze supply. The forfeited quotas will be redirected to projects deemed of 'national interest,' including local processing and value addition. (Mining Weekly)

The major producers affected include China's CMOC (the world's largest cobalt producer), Glencore, Eurasian Resources Group, and Huayou Cobalt. ARECOMS warned it could withdraw quotas entirely from companies that fail to export allocated volumes, transfer quotas to third parties, or process artisanal material without authorisation. (Mining Weekly)
sections.whyItMatters: Congo is turning export quotas into an industrial-policy lever — not just restricting volume but redirecting it toward domestic processing. That tightens the cobalt bottleneck for EV battery supply chains and gives Kinshasa negotiating leverage over both Chinese miners and Western automakers.

sections.heading: Google Caps Meta's Gemini Access — Compute Rationing Goes Public
sections.body:
Google told Meta it couldn't fully meet the Gemini computing capacity Meta sought in March 2026, capping access due to insufficient supply. The restrictions disrupted several of Meta's internal AI projects, leading the company to tell staff to use AI tokens more efficiently. (Financial Times via Memeburn)

On May 17, Google formalized compute-based usage limits on Gemini Apps broadly. Access now scales with available capacity, not willingness to pay. Google Cloud crossed $20 billion in quarterly revenue in Q1 2026, but CEO Sundar Pichai acknowledged the constraint: 'We are compute-constrained in the near term. Our Cloud revenue would have been higher if we were able to meet the demand.' Google's committed-but-undelivered cloud contracts jumped from $240 billion to $460 billion in a single quarter. (Memeburn)

Meta's response: on April 8, it launched Muse Spark, its first proprietary closed-source model — a sharp departure from years of open-source commitment. Meta claims Muse Spark achieves comparable capability with 10x less compute than Llama 4 Maverick. (Memeburn)
sections.whyItMatters: When a company spending $14.3 billion on AI can't buy enough compute, the constraint isn't capital — it's physical infrastructure. The compute shortage is now reshaping corporate strategy: Meta abandoned open-source principles because it couldn't secure supply from a competitor. This is supply-chain geopolitics at the data center level.

sections.heading: Chinese AI and Chip Firms Fuel Onshore IPO Rebound
sections.body:
Chinese technology companies raised $3.1 billion from onshore stock market listings in the first half of 2026 — more than 5x the volume in the same period last year, according to LSEG data cited by Reuters. Nearly 50 more companies, including robotics startups and semiconductor firms, have applied for IPOs in Shanghai and Shenzhen, with planned fundraising of at least 126.1 billion yuan ($18.7 billion). (Memeburn/Reuters)

The biggest name in the pipeline: ChangXin Memory Technologies (CXMT), planning a 29.5 billion yuan Shanghai IPO that would be China's largest listing this year. Zhipu AI, which raised HK$4.35 billion in Hong Kong in January, is aiming for a 15 billion yuan STAR Market listing. Baidu's chip unit Kunlunxin is awaiting approval for a $2 billion Hong Kong listing while also planning a domestic float. (Memeburn/Reuters)

China's securities regulator said it would support listings for companies in 'future industries,' including quantum technology, embodied AI, biomanufacturing, and other hard-tech sectors. The Shanghai Stock Exchange issued new guidance requiring at least one large-model product already launched and used at scale. (Xinhua; SSE)

Recent listings show strong appetite: SJ Semiconductor has risen more than 8x from IPO price, while Semight Instruments jumped nearly 28x. (Reuters via Memeburn)
sections.whyItMatters: Beijing is turning domestic capital markets into an industrial-policy instrument for tech self-reliance. If CXMT, Zhipu, and Kunlunxin list at home, China's most strategic AI and chip firms gain a funding channel tied to national priorities — and domestic investors, not foreign ones, capture the upside. The sector becomes more political, and more resilient to US capital-market pressure.

sections.heading: Trump-Kazakhstan Tungsten Deal Faces Conflict-of-Interest Scrutiny
sections.body:
A $1.6 billion US-backed mining project in Kazakhstan for tungsten — a critical mineral where China controls roughly 80% of global supply — is facing scrutiny after a New York Times investigation detailed investment links between the families of Donald Trump and Kazakhstan's leadership. (NYT via MSN; Forbes via MSN)

Separately, Bloomberg reported on an Australian tungsten mine reopening — a mine whose fortunes have 'risen and fallen through more than a century of conflicts' — as Western countries seek alternative supply chains. (Bloomberg)

The deals reflect a broader US push to break dependence on Chinese critical minerals. The Kazakhstan agreement and the Australian mine revival both aim to secure non-Chinese tungsten supply for defense and industrial applications. But the Trump family's reported investment ties to the Kazakh deal have raised conflict-of-interest questions about whether national security mineral strategy is intersecting with presidential family business interests. (NYT; MSN)
sections.whyItMatters: The US is scrambling to build alternative critical mineral supply chains — but if the vehicles for doing so become politically compromised, the strategy loses credibility with allies and partners. Tungsten is essential for munitions, electronics, and industrial tooling. China's dominance isn't just a supply problem; it's a leverage problem.

sections.heading: China Pressures Japan With Bombers, Detentions, and Rare Earth Curbs
sections.body:
The New York Times reported that Beijing's pressure campaign against Japan now includes flying bombers near Japanese airspace, detaining Japanese businesspeople, and restricting rare earth exports — combining military intimidation with economic coercion. (NYT)

The rare earth restrictions are the most strategically significant element for the tech supply chain. China controls roughly 60% of global rare earth mining and 90% of processing. Previous short-duration export restrictions on gallium and germanium in 2023–2024 demonstrated Beijing's willingness to use mineral supply as a foreign policy lever. Applying similar pressure to Japan — a major semiconductor and electronics manufacturer — tests the escalation ladder. (NYT)

Japan has been building alternative rare earth supply chains since the 2010 Senkaku incident, when China briefly cut off rare earth exports. Progress has been real but slow: diversification away from Chinese processing remains incomplete, particularly for heavy rare earths used in high-performance magnets. (NYT)
sections.whyItMatters: China is running a three-track pressure campaign against Japan — military, personal, and economic. The rare earth dimension tests whether Japan's decade-plus diversification effort has created enough resilience to absorb coercion, or whether the dependency is still acute enough to give Beijing real leverage.

bottomLine: July 1, 2026 marks a convergence point where the hardware layer of tech competition shifted meaningfully. Meituan proved that frontier-scale AI models can be trained on domestic Chinese chips — the most credible signal yet that US GPU export controls have a shrinking window of effectiveness. FERC's unanimous order to accelerate data center grid connections shows the US trying to clear infrastructure bottlenecks on its side, but the $460 billion gap between Google's committed and delivered cloud capacity reveals how far physical reality lags behind capital allocation. Congo is weaponizing cobalt quotas. China is running a three-track pressure campaign on Japan. And Beijing is turning its IPO market into a self-reliance funding vehicle. The common thread: states are treating supply chains as strategic instruments, not market outcomes — and today, several of those instruments moved simultaneously.
sources: SiliconANGLE, The Next Web, VentureBeat, AP News, Southern Maryland Chronicle, Dallas Morning News, Mining Weekly (Reuters), Memeburn (Reuters/LSEG), Financial Times, Xinhua, Shanghai Stock Exchange, New York Times, MSN, Bloomberg
